rules.message: `updateMany`/`deleteMany` called with no `where:` clause anywhere in the enclosing function â€” a whole-table write, not a filtered subset. Add a `where:` filter that scopes the write, or mark a genuinely full-table write explicitly. Suppress a vetted case with `// no-where-ok`.

`skip`/`take` pagination used with no `orderBy` in the enclosing function â€” without a stable sort, page boundaries can shift between requests (rows skipped or duplicated across pages). Add an `orderBy:` on a unique or otherwise stable column. Suppress a vetted case with `// pagination-ok`.

`new PrismaClient()` constructed inside a function that looks like a request handler (references `req`/`res`/`ctx`/`request`/`reply`) â€” a fresh client per request exhausts the database connection pool under load. Hoist a single client to a module-level singleton instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// prisma-client-ok`.

A network call (`fetch`/`axios`/`got`) appears in the same function as a `$transaction(` â€” holding a database transaction open across a network round-trip extends lock hold time, and stalls with it if the external call stalls. Move the network call outside the `$transaction` block. Suppress a vetted case with `// tx-egress-ok`.

A database write (`create`/`update`/`delete`/`upsert`) on a DB-client-shaped receiver (`prisma`/`db`/`tx`/`client`/`repo`) whose promise is neither awaited, returned, nor chained â€” a failed write looks identical to a successful one. Await the call (or return it, or attach `.then()`/`.catch()`). Suppress a vetted case with `// unawaited-ok`.

A `take`/`limit` pagination size is read directly from user input with no upper-bound clamp â€” a client can force the server to load and serialize an unbounded number of rows (memory/CPU exhaustion). Clamp it against a maximum (e.g. `Math.min(MAX_PAGE_SIZE, Number(req.query.limit))`). Suppress a vetted case with `// limit-ok`.

A `findFirst`/`findOne`/`findUnique` read followed by `.create(` with no `connectOrCreate` or `upsert` â€” a check-then-act race lets two concurrent requests both pass and create a duplicate row. Add a unique constraint and replace the check-then-act with an upsert/`connectOrCreate`; wrapping the read+write in a `$transaction` alone does NOT close this race at the database's default READ COMMITTED isolation level â€” two concurrent transactions can both read empty and both insert. Suppress a vetted case with `// find-create-ok`.

A money-named identifier (`price`/`amount`/`balance`/`fee`/`cost`) compared with `==`/`===`/`!=`/`!==` against a float literal (e.g. `price === 19.99`) â€” floating-point rounding error makes strict equality on monetary values unreliable. Represent money as integer minor units (cents) or a decimal library. Suppress a vetted case with `// money-ok`.

A database write (`create`/`update`/`delete`/`upsert`/`updateMany`/`deleteMany`) appears in the same function as an empty `catch {}` block â€” the write's failure is caught and silently discarded, indistinguishable from success. Log or rethrow the error instead of swallowing it. Suppress a vetted case with `// empty-catch-ok`.

Two independent write calls from different verb families â€” `create`/`createMany`/`insert` and `update`/`updateMany`/`delete`/`deleteMany`/`upsert` â€” appear in the same function with no transaction wrapping either. This is a co-occurrence heuristic, not proof the two writes depend on each other, but when they do: a failure between the two calls leaves partial state, since the first write already committed and nothing rolls it back if the second throws. Wrap both writes in `$transaction(...)` (or an explicit `BEGIN`/`COMMIT` transaction), or replace the pair with a single write. Suppress a vetted case (e.g. two genuinely independent writes) with `// multi-write-tx-ok`.

A read (`findUnique`/`findFirst`/`findOne`/`findById`) is followed, elsewhere in the same function, by an update that recomputes a field as `<ident> + 1`/`<ident> - 1` â€” read-then-arithmetic-update is not atomic: two concurrent requests can both read the same starting value and both write the same incremented result, silently losing one of the two increments. This is a co-occurrence heuristic, not proof â€” any `field: value + 1`-shaped object entry in the same function trips it, even one unrelated to the read (e.g. a `take: cursor + 1` pagination bound). Use the ORM's atomic increment/decrement (e.g. Prisma's `{ increment: 1 }`/`{ decrement: 1 }`), a DB-side `$inc`, or a `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` row lock instead of read-then-write arithmetic. Suppress a vetted case with `// atomic-counter-ok`.

A connection acquired via `pool.connect(`/`getConnection(`/`acquireConnection(` is never released (`.release(`/`.destroy(`/`.end(`) or returned (`return conn`/`connection`/`client`) anywhere in this function â€” under load, each unreleased connection stays checked out of the pool, and once the pool is exhausted every later request stalls waiting for one to free up. Release it in a `finally` block, use a `using`/`await using` declaration (recognized, does not fire), or use the pool's single-shot query shortcut instead of a manual acquire/release pair. Note the limitation: this only sees the current function's own body â€” a release happening inside a helper function called from here is invisible to this check and still fires; verify before refactoring, or suppress a vetted case with `// connection-release-ok`.
